topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and dusty, smelling of aged paper and forgotten lavender. Liam had been tasked with clearing out some boxes for his grandmother, a chore he’d dreaded. Tucked beneath a moth-eaten quilt, he found a shoebox labeled simply, "Schoolwork." Inside, the contents were a cascade of meticulously organized notebooks, each filled with neat, looping handwriting. Every test, every essay, every homework assignment from elementary school through high school was present. A cold prickle traced a line down Liam’s spine as he flipped through a stack of algebra proofs. It was too much. He sat back on his heels, his breath catching in his throat, the silence of the attic suddenly amplifying the frantic drumming in his chest. He looked around the shadowed space, suddenly aware of being watched, even though he knew he was alone.

[story 2]

"Mom, what is all this?" Sarah asked, her voice tight with confusion as she held up a heavy binder overflowing with graded papers. Her mother, usually breezy and cheerful, stood by the doorway, a faint, almost imperceptible tension around her mouth. Sarah’s hands trembled slightly as she realized the sheer volume of documentation: every paper, every worksheet, every completed project from the last decade. She ran a finger over a pristine essay on the causes of the Civil War, the ink stark against the cream paper. A strange, hollow feeling settled in her stomach, a heavy weight that made her shoulders slump. She wanted to ask a question, to demand an explanation, but the words caught in her throat, lodging there like grit. She just stared at the collection, feeling profoundly adrift.

[story 3]

I found the box hidden behind the loose floorboard in the spare room. It wasn't the contents that made my palms sweat; it was the sheer completeness of it. My mother had kept every single piece of schoolwork. From kindergarten drawings to final exams, it was an exhaustive record of my entire academic life. I picked up a history report from sophomore year, the handwriting identical to mine, yet somehow alien. A dizzying sense of wrongness washed over me. I dropped the paper, the sound surprisingly loud in the quiet room. My jaw tightened, and I began pacing the small space, my movements jerky and uneven. Every time I stopped, I’d look back at the box, a growing sense of apprehension making the hairs on my arms stand straight up.
